summary: AI compute control plane across clouds, clusters, and accelerators.
editor_note: GPU scarcity creates a practical kind of lock-in: the job goes where capacity exists. SkyPilot turns Kubernetes, Slurm, on-prem clusters, and 20+ clouds into one control plane for AI work. Unlike another neocloud, the bet is neutrality over owning supply. The risk is whether infra teams want a broker in the middle of production workloads.

product_evidence.claim: The official site says SkyPilot manages AI compute across Kubernetes, Slurm, VMs, on-prem clusters, and 20+ clouds under one interface.
The official docs expose concrete workflows for launching, managing, and scaling AI workloads across many cloud and cluster backends.
The GitHub repository describes SkyPilot as an AI compute platform for frontier teams and exposes the open-source project surface.

market_context.primary_user: Frontier AI teams, ML infrastructure engineers, research labs, and platform teams managing scarce GPU capacity.
market_context.differentiation: SkyPilot abstracts fragmented compute into a scheduler and control plane across clouds and clusters rather than locking teams into a single neocloud or hyperscaler.
market_context.why_now: AI teams are mixing reservations, burst capacity, Kubernetes, Slurm, and new accelerator types as GPU supply fragments across providers.

risks.claim: The falsifiable risk is whether production AI teams trust a neutral orchestration layer for quota, failover, monitoring, and workload placement across critical compute pools.
risks.basis: VentureDex assessment from the official site, docs, GitHub repository, and seed coverage.
